deep_analysis.executive_intro:
The FTSE 100 screen is broadly neutral: 70 names sit on Hold, with only four Strong Buys and four Buys against 15 Avoids and seven with insufficient data. The highest-conviction quantitative ideas cluster in cyclical, commodity-linked sectors — two Basic Materials names (Rio Tinto, Endeavour Mining), one Energy (Shell), and two Consumer Cyclicals (Barratt Redrow, Howden Joinery) — suggesting the models are finding value where earnings and multiples are depressed by macro or sector cycles rather than in defensive staples. All top candidates are new signals (one week at rating) with conviction scores of only 34–45%, so the screen is flagging fresh opportunities rather than sustained, high-confidence themes. A key caution: data quality is high (19/20 metrics) but several models report missing operating cash flow, NCAV, and earnings-quality inputs, and every top pick carries a "new" stability label — treat these as screening leads, not fully diligenced positions.

Rio Tinto Group (RIO.L) — Basic Materials, Strong Buy. Quantitative models passed 10 of 22, with composite score 74% and sector-relative 77%, clearing all five factor families (cheapness, quality, dividend, GARP, risk). Key passes include Graham Enterprising, Quality Value, Buffett Quality, High Dividend Yield, Magic Formula, Acquirer's Multiple, and Financial Health, supported by P/E 14.6, P/B 2.4, yield 4.5%, ROE 16.4%, and D/E 35%. Sector concentration risk is material: two of five top picks are Basic Materials miners. Verdict: accumulate — broad multi-model value and income support at reasonable leverage, though conviction is only 45% on a new signal and Piotroski F-Score failed at 3/9 with negative earnings growth flagged.

Endeavour Mining plc (EDV.L) — Basic Materials, Strong Buy. The strongest model tally at 11/22 (composite 74%, sector-relative 67%), passing all five families including FCF Yield (17.5%), Lynch PEG (PEG 0.13, growth 105.7%), Quality Value, Buffett Quality, Economic Moat, Magic Formula, Acquirer's Multiple, and Financial Health; metrics show P/E 13.9, P/B 3.6, yield 2.9%, ROE 31.1%. Pairs with RIO.L in Basic Materials, doubling commodity-cycle exposure in a concentrated top-five list. Verdict: watchlist — exceptional GARP and cash-flow scores, but P/B 3.6 fails Graham Enterprising, current ratio 1.11 fails defensive screens, and 105.7% growth may not be sustainable through a gold-price downturn.

Barratt Redrow plc (BTRW.L) — Consumer Cyclical, Strong Buy. Eight of 22 models passed (composite 69%, sector-relative 75%) across cheapness, dividend, GARP, and risk — notably Schloss Low P/B (P/B 0.51, D/E 3%), Deep Value (EV/EBITDA 6.9), Lynch/Neff PEGY, High Dividend Yield, Dividend Growth, Composite Value, and Financial Health; headline metrics are P/E 18.7, P/B 0.5, yield 6.1%, but ROE is only 2.8% and FCF is negative (-£77m). Shares Consumer Cyclical exposure with Howden Joinery. Verdict: watchlist — deep book-value discount and strong balance sheet (D/E 3%, coverage 4.5x), but sub-3% ROE, negative FCF yield (-2.0%), failed Quality Value/Buffett Quality, and Piotroski F-Score 4/9 point to earnings quality concerns in a housing-cycle name.

Shell plc (SHEL.L) — Energy, Strong Buy. Nine of 22 models passed (composite 62%, sector-relative 81%) on cheapness, dividend, GARP, and risk, including Graham Enterprising (P/E 12.5, P/B 1.32, D/E 43%, growth 26.6%), FCF Yield (9.3%), Lynch PEG, Neff PEGY, Dividend Growth, Magic Formula, Acquirer's Multiple, Dreman Contrarian, and Financial Health; yield 3.8%, ROE 10.7%. Adds a third cyclical/commodity leg alongside the two miners. Verdict: accumulate — strongest sector-relative score (81%) with cheap multiples and robust FCF, though quality screens fail on ROE/margins and Piotroski F-Score is 3/9, consistent with energy cyclicality rather than franchise quality.

Howden Joinery Group Plc (HWDN.L) — Consumer Cyclical, Buy. Seven of 22 models passed (composite 62%, sector-relative 63%) across all five families: FCF Yield (5.1%), Neff PEGY (PEGY 0.05), Quality Value, Buffett Quality (ROE 23.1%, margin 11.1%), Dividend Growth, Magic Formula, and Financial Health; P/E 15.9, P/B 3.5, yield 2.8%. Second Consumer Cyclical name, reinforcing UK housing/RMI cyclicality in the list. Verdict: watchlist — solid quality metrics and financial health (coverage 15.2x), but only a Buy (not Strong Buy), price below 50- and 200-day MAs with weakening momentum, and Lynch PEG failed at 1.73.

The JSON contains no qualitative research overlays (all `research_verdict`, `research_risk_level`, and `research_rationale` fields are null), so the following risks are not captured by the quantitative screen and must be assessed separately.

Rio Tinto (RIO.L): High exposure to iron ore and aluminium prices, China demand, and ESG/regulatory scrutiny over mining permits and emissions; commodity downturns can compress earnings faster than trailing P/E suggests. Governance and jurisdictional risk (operations across Australia, Mongolia, Guinea, etc.) is not modelled. Pension and off-balance-sheet liabilities are not in the payload.

Endeavour Mining (EDV.L): Gold-miner cyclicality and West African operational/political risk (Burkina Faso, Ivory Coast, Senegal); reserve depletion and cap-ex cycles are not screened. Rapid 105.7% earnings growth in the models may reflect commodity price or one-off gains rather than repeatable FCF. Governance and country-risk data insufficient in JSON.

Barratt Redrow (BTRW.L): UK housebuilder highly sensitive to interest rates, mortgage availability, Help-to-Buy/policy shifts, and land-bank impairment risk; post-merger integration (Barratt/Redrow) is not captured. Negative FCF and 2.8% ROE suggest the 6.1% yield may face cut risk in a prolonged housing downturn. Pension deficits and warranty/cladding liabilities are not in the data.

Shell (SHEL.L): Oil & gas price volatility, energy-transition/stranded-asset debate, and climate litigation/regulatory risk; dividend policy tied to commodity cycles despite passing dividend screens. Geopolitical exposure (Russia exit legacy, LNG markets) and capex allocation between fossil and renewables not screened. ROE 10.7% and thin margins (7.0%) signal cyclical earnings, not moat quality.

Howden Joinery (HWDN.L): UK kitchen/RMI demand tied to housing transactions and consumer confidence; not a deep-value candidate (P/B 3.5) despite passing quality screens. Leverage at D/E 59% is elevated versus peers in the screen. Competitive pressure from trade distributors and cyclical slowdown risk not modelled.

Cross-cutting screen gaps: Piotroski F-Scores are weak across all five names (3–6/9); Earnings Quality models failed on missing OCF for every candidate; Graham Net-Net failed on missing NCAV for all; and all signals are one week old with low conviction — none have proven signal persistence.

Names worth deeper research: Rio Tinto (RIO.L) — balance cyclical risk against multi-model value and 4.5% yield; Shell (SHEL.L) — validate FCF durability and transition capex against 9.3% FCF yield; Endeavour Mining (EDV.L) — stress-test 105.7% growth and West African risk against 17.5% FCF yield.
